votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the three conditions of the Coq specification: non-emptiness, membership of m in the list, and the property that all elements in the list are less than or equal to m.
The Python code correctly implements the three conditions of the Coq specification: non-empty list, membership of m, and m being the maximum element.
The Python code correctly implements all three conditions of the Coq specification: non-emptiness, membership of m in l, and the property that all elements in l are less than or equal to m.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification: it checks that the list is non-empty (l <> []), that m is in the list (In m l), and that all elements x in the list satisfy x <= m (forall x, In x l -> x <= m). All three conditions are properly translated and combined with logical AND.

The Python code correctly implements all three conditions of the Coq spec: (1) l <> [] is checked via len(l) > 0, (2) In m l is checked via m in l, and (3) forall x, In x l -> x <= m is checked via all(x <= m for x in l). All three conditions produce the same boolean result for any concrete list of natural numbers and candidate maximum m.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification: it checks that the list is non-empty (l <> []), that m is in the list (In m l), and that all elements x in the list satisfy x <= m (forall x, In x l -> x <= m). All three conditions are combined with logical AND, matching the Coq conjunction.
Checks non-empty list, membership of m, and that every element is <= m; this matches the Coq spec for lists of natural numbers.
Checks non-empty list, membership of m, and that every element in l is <= m, matching the Coq spec on lists of naturals.
Checks non-emptiness, membership of m, and that every element is <= m, matching the Coq spec on lists of naturals.
